title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Note



Write a short introduction for the reader that sets the scene. Cover the following points in your own words:

You have a website and your landing page includes a place where visitors can sign up to receive your newsletter.
You want to test which of two possible “calls to action” (CTAs) leads to more sign-ups. CTA A reads “Sign up for our newsletter here!” and CTA B reads “Stay up to date by signing up!” (you are welcome to change the language of the CTAs).
You will evaluate which CTA performs better by running an A/B test. Explain what an A/B test is: visitors are randomly assigned to see one of the two CTAs, and you compare sign-up rates between the two groups.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Note



Write a short introduction for the reader that sets the scene. Cover the following points in your own words:

You have a website and your landing page includes a place where visitors can sign up to receive your newsletter.
You want to test which of two possible “calls to action” (CTAs) leads to more sign-ups. CTA A reads “Sign up for our newsletter here!” and CTA B reads “Stay up to date by signing up!” (you are welcome to change the language of the CTAs).
You will evaluate which CTA performs better by running an A/B test. Explain what an A/B test is: visitors are randomly assigned to see one of the two CTAs, and you compare sign-up rates between the two groups.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
section: The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
text:
The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem






Note



In this section, connect the A/B test to a formal statistical framework. Address each of the following ideas:

Each visitor either signs up or does not, so we can model each visitor’s outcome as a draw from a Bernoulli distribution with parameter \(\pi\), the probability of signing up.
Because the CTA a visitor sees may influence their probability of signing up, we allow the parameter to differ across groups: visitors who see CTA A have sign-up probability \(\pi_A\), and visitors who see CTA B have sign-up probability \(\pi_B\). Each visitor can either sign up (1) or not (0).
The quantity we care about is \(\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B\), the difference in sign-up rates between the two CTAs.
Our estimator of \(\theta\) is the difference in sample means, \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\). (Since each observation is either 0 or 1, the sample mean is simply the proportion of visitors who signed up — so \(\bar{X}_A = \hat\pi_A\) and \(\bar{X}_B = \hat\pi_B\).)

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Simulating Data






Note




Write a brief paragraph for the reader explaining that in the real world we would not know the true values of \(\pi_A\) and \(\pi_B\), but for the purpose of this exercise we will set them ourselves so that we can study the behavior of our estimator when we know the truth.
Suppose \(\pi_A = 0.22\) and \(\pi_B = 0.18\), so the true difference is \(\theta = 0.04\).
Using a language of your choice, simulate 1,000 draws from each of the two Bernoulli distributions (2,000 total). Store the results so you can use them in the sections that follow.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Law of Large Numbers






Note




Write an introductory sentence or two explaining the Law of Large Numbers to the reader: as the sample size grows, the sample mean converges to the population mean. Connect this back to our estimator — the LLN is what gives us confidence that \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\) will be close to the true \(\theta\) when our sample is large enough.
Then demonstrate this visually. Using the simulated data from the previous section, compute the element-wise differences between the CTA A draws and the CTA B draws to get a vector of 1,000 differences. Calculate the cumulative average of that vector (i.e., the running mean after 1 draw, after 2 draws, …, after 1,000 draws) and plot it against the number of observations.
The plot should show a noisy average when only a few observations are included that settles down and converges toward the true difference of 0.04 as the sample size grows.
After the plot, write a few sentences explaining what the reader should take away from it.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Bootstrap Standard Errors






Note




Now that we “trust” our estimator, the next natural question is: how precise is it? We would like to report not just a point estimate of \(\theta\) but also a confidence interval that communicates our uncertainty.
Write a short introduction explaining the idea of bootstrapping to the reader. The bootstrap is a resampling method that lets us estimate the variability of a statistic without relying on a closed-form formula. We resample (with replacement) from our observed data many times, compute the statistic of interest on each resample, and use the standard deviation of those resampled statistics as our estimate of the standard error.
Then carry out the following steps:

From your 1,000 CTA A draws and 1,000 CTA B draws, draw a bootstrap resample of size 1,000 (with replacement) from each group. Compute \(\hat\theta^* = \bar{X}_A^* - \bar{X}_B^*\) for that resample.
Repeat step 1 a total of 1,000 times to produce a vector of 1,000 bootstrapped estimates of \(\theta\).
Compute the standard deviation of those 1,000 values. This is your bootstrapped standard error.
Compare it to the analytical standard error. For the difference in means of two independent Bernoulli samples, the standard error formula is:


\[
SE(\hat\theta) = \sqrt{\frac{\hat\pi_A(1 - \hat\pi_A)}{n_A} + \frac{\hat\pi_B(1 - \hat\pi_B)}{n_B}}
\]
where \(\hat\pi_A\) and \(\hat\pi_B\) are the sample proportions and \(n_A\) and \(n_B\) are the sample sizes.

Report both the bootstrapped standard error and the analytical standard error and comment on how close they are to each other. Then use the bootstrapped standard error to construct a 95% confidence interval for \(\theta\) and interpret it for the reader.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Central Limit Theorem






Note




Write a short introduction explaining the Central Limit Theorem to the reader: regardless of the shape of the underlying population distribution, the sampling distribution of the sample mean (and therefore of the difference in sample means) becomes approximately Normal as the sample size grows.
Then demonstrate this visually by creating four histograms, one for each of the following sample sizes: \(n \in \{25, 50, 100, 500\}\).
For each sample size \(n\):

Draw \(n\) observations from the CTA A distribution and \(n\) observations from the CTA B distribution.
Compute \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\).
Repeat steps 1–2 a total of 1,000 times to produce 1,000 values of \(\hat\theta\).
Plot a histogram of those 1,000 values.

Arrange the four histograms so they can be compared easily (e.g., in a 2x2 grid with consistent x-axis scales).
After the plots, write a few sentences explaining what the reader should observe: at small sample sizes the histogram may look rough or asymmetric, but as \(n\) increases the distribution of \(\hat\theta\) becomes smoother and more bell-shaped, illustrating the Central Limit Theorem in action.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Hypothesis Testing






Note




Now that we have seen the CLT in action — that the sampling distribution of \(\hat\theta\) is approximately Normal for large samples — we can use this result to perform a formal hypothesis test.
Write a paragraph explaining what a hypothesis test is to the reader. We set up a null hypothesis \(H_0: \theta = 0\) (i.e., the two CTAs produce the same sign-up rate) and an alternative hypothesis \(H_1: \theta \neq 0\). The goal is to assess whether the data provide enough evidence to reject \(H_0\).
Then explain why we standardize \(\hat\theta\) to form a test statistic. We compute:

\[
z = \frac{\hat\theta - 0}{SE(\hat\theta)}
\]

Walk the reader through the logic of this standardization step by step:

By the CLT, we know that \(\hat\theta\) is approximately Normal for large samples. Under \(H_0\), the mean of that distribution is 0.
When we divide a Normal random variable by its standard deviation, we get a standard Normal \(N(0,1)\). So under \(H_0\), \(z \;\dot\sim\; N(0,1)\).
This means we know the distribution of our test statistic under the null, which is exactly what we need to compute a p-value.

Explain to the reader why this is sometimes called a “t-test” even though the test statistic follows an approximate Normal distribution, not a t-distribution. The classical t-test arises when the data are drawn from a Normal distribution and the variance must be estimated — the ratio then follows an exact t-distribution. In our setting the data are Bernoulli (not Normal), so there is no exact t-distribution result. The CLT gives us approximate Normality, and since we estimate the SE from the data, what we are really doing is closer to a z-test. In practice, for large samples the t-distribution and the standard Normal are nearly identical, so the distinction has little practical consequence — but it is important to understand why.
Using your simulated data, compute the test statistic and its associated p-value (using the standard Normal distribution as the reference). Interpret the result for the reader.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The T-Test as a Regression






Note




It turns out that the two-sample test we just ran is mathematically equivalent to a simple linear regression. Understanding this connection is useful because it means the same machinery — and the same software — that you use for regression can also perform hypothesis tests, and it generalizes naturally to more complex experimental designs.
Explain the setup to the reader. Stack the CTA A and CTA B data into a single dataset with two columns: an outcome \(Y_i\) (1 if the visitor signed up, 0 otherwise) and an indicator \(D_i\) (1 if the visitor saw CTA A, 0 if they saw CTA B). Then fit the regression:

\[
Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 D_i + \varepsilon_i
\]

Walk through the interpretation of the coefficients:

\(\beta_0\) is the expected value of \(Y_i\) when \(D_i = 0\) — i.e., the mean of the B group, which estimates \(\pi_B\).
\(\beta_0 + \beta_1\) is the expected value of \(Y_i\) when \(D_i = 1\) — i.e., the mean of the A group, which estimates \(\pi_A\).
Therefore \(\beta_1 = \pi_A - \pi_B = \theta\), and the OLS estimate \(\hat\beta_1\) is numerically identical to \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\).

Fit this regression using your simulated data. Report the coefficient estimate \(\hat\beta_1\), its standard error, its t-statistic, and its p-value. Compare these to the values you computed in the previous section using the two-sample approach — they should match (up to a small difference in how the standard error is computed; standard OLS assumes a single pooled variance, while the two-sample formula we used assumes separate variances for each group).
Explain to the reader why this equivalence matters: the regression framework makes it easy to add controls, interactions, or additional treatment arms, all while producing the same inference in the simple case.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Problem with Peeking






Note




Your boss is impatient. Rather than waiting until all 1,000 visitors have been assigned to each group, she wants to run the hypothesis test after every 100 visitors per group — peeking at the results after 100, 200, 300, …, up to 1,000 visitors per group (10 tests in total). If any of those tests comes back significant at the \(\alpha = 0.05\) level, she wants to declare a winner and stop the experiment early.
Explain to the reader why this is problematic under classical frequentist statistics. The key issue is that a single test at \(\alpha = 0.05\) has a 5% chance of a false positive (rejecting \(H_0\) when it is actually true). But when you conduct multiple tests on accumulating data, each peek is another opportunity to get a false positive. The overall probability of at least one false rejection across all the peeks is much higher than 5%.
Demonstrate this with a simulation. The idea is as follows:

Set up a world where the null hypothesis is true: \(\pi_A = \pi_B = 0.20\) (i.e., \(\theta = 0\), there is no difference between the CTAs).
Simulate a single experiment: draw 1,000 observations for group A and 1,000 for group B. After every 100 observations per group (at \(n = 100, 200, \ldots, 1000\)), compute the z-statistic and p-value. Record whether any of the 10 p-values falls below 0.05.
Repeat step 2 a total of 10,000 times to simulate 10,000 experiments.
Compute the fraction of those 10,000 experiments in which at least one peek produced a significant result. This is the empirical false positive rate under peeking.

Compare this to the nominal 5% false positive rate of a single test. Report the result and explain to the reader why peeking inflates the Type I error rate and what this means in practice for running A/B tests.

The Top Contenders
As of April 2026, the field of 48 teams is set. Based on current FIFA rankings and betting markets, three nations stand above the rest:

Spain (The Favorites): Currently leading prediction markets with a 16-18% win probability. Their technical consistency remains unmatched.
France: The world #1 ranked team. With a peak-age Kylian Mbappé, they are the most formidable squad on paper.
England: Boasting a deep roster of elite talent, they are widely tipped to finally break their “near-miss” streak.

Why Spain Will Win
Spain is my pick to lift the trophy on July 19, 2026. Here is why they have the edge:

Reigning European Form: Their success in recent UEFA competitions has built a winning culture.
Tactical Identity: Their possession-based system is perfect for navigating a grueling 104-match tournament.
Squad Depth: Unlike teams reliant on a single superstar, Spain’s strength is in a system that remains effective regardless of personnel changes.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Imagine you run a website with a newsletter signup box on the landing page. You are considering two versions of the call to action, or CTA. CTA A says “Sign up for our newsletter here!” and CTA B says “Stay up to date by signing up!” The goal is to figure out which message leads to more newsletter sign-ups.
A natural way to answer that question is with an A/B test. Visitors are randomly assigned to see one of the two CTAs, and then we compare the sign-up rates across the two groups. Random assignment matters because it helps make the two groups comparable. If one CTA gets a higher sign-up rate, we have a stronger case that the wording itself caused the difference.
In this post, I use a simulated A/B test to walk through several core ideas from classical frequentist statistics: estimation, the Law of Large Numbers, bootstrap standard errors, the Central Limit Theorem, hypothesis testing, the regression interpretation of a two-sample test, and why “peeking” at results can get you into trouble.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Imagine you run a website with a newsletter signup box on the landing page. You are considering two versions of the call to action, or CTA. CTA A says “Sign up for our newsletter here!” and CTA B says “Stay up to date by signing up!” The goal is to figure out which message leads to more newsletter sign-ups.
A natural way to answer that question is with an A/B test. Visitors are randomly assigned to see one of the two CTAs, and then we compare the sign-up rates across the two groups. Random assignment matters because it helps make the two groups comparable. If one CTA gets a higher sign-up rate, we have a stronger case that the wording itself caused the difference.
In this post, I use a simulated A/B test to walk through several core ideas from classical frequentist statistics: estimation, the Law of Large Numbers, bootstrap standard errors, the Central Limit Theorem, hypothesis testing, the regression interpretation of a two-sample test, and why “peeking” at results can get you into trouble.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
section: The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
text:
The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
For each visitor, the outcome is binary: either they sign up for the newsletter or they do not. That means each observation can be modeled as a draw from a Bernoulli distribution, where the parameter \(\) is the probability of signing up.
Because the CTA wording may affect behavior, I allow the sign-up probability to differ across groups. Visitors who see CTA A have sign-up probability \(_A\), and visitors who see CTA B have sign-up probability \(_B\).
The quantity I care about is the difference in sign-up rates:
\[ = _A - _B \]
A natural estimator is the difference in sample means:
\[ = {X}_A - {X}_B \]
Because the outcomes are 0 or 1, each sample mean is also a sample proportion.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Simulating Data
In a real A/B test, we would not know the true values of \(_A\) and \(_B\). For this exercise, I set them myself so I can study how the estimator behaves when the truth is known.
Suppose the true sign-up rates are \(_A = 0.22\) and \(_B = 0.18\). Then the true difference is:
\[ = 0.22 - 0.18 = 0.04 \]

sim_summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "Group": ["CTA A", "CTA B"],
    "True rate": [pct(pi_A), pct(pi_B)],
    "Observed rate": [pct(pi_A_hat), pct(pi_B_hat)],
    "Signups": [A.sum(), B.sum()],
    "Visitors": [len(A), len(B)]
})

display(sim_summary)
print(f"True difference: {pct(theta_true)}")
print(f"Observed difference: {pct(theta_hat)}")

Observed rate

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Law of Large Numbers
The Law of Large Numbers says that as the sample size grows, the sample mean converges to the population mean. In this setting, that gives us confidence that \(\) will get closer to the true \(\) as more observations arrive.

diffs = A - B
running_mean = np.cumsum(diffs) / np.arange(1, n + 1)

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
plt.plot(np.arange(1, n + 1), running_mean)
plt.axhline(theta_true, linestyle="--")
plt.title("The Law of Large Numbers in an A/B Test")
plt.xlabel("Number of paired observations")
plt.ylabel(r"Running estimate of $\hat{\theta}$")
plt.show()








At the beginning, the estimate is noisy. As the sample size grows, the running average settles down and moves closer to the true difference of 0.04.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Bootstrap Standard Errors
A point estimate is useful, but it does not tell us how uncertain we are. The bootstrap is a resampling method that estimates variability by repeatedly resampling from the observed data with replacement.

B_boot = 1000
boot_thetas = np.empty(B_boot)

for i in range(B_boot):
    A_star = np.random.choice(A, size=n, replace=True)
    B_star = np.random.choice(B, size=n, replace=True)
    boot_thetas[i] = A_star.mean() - B_star.mean()

boot_se = boot_thetas.std(ddof=1)

analytic_se = np.sqrt(
    (pi_A_hat * (1 - pi_A_hat)) / n +
    (pi_B_hat * (1 - pi_B_hat)) / n
)

ci_lower = theta_hat - 1.96 * boot_se
ci_upper = theta_hat + 1.96 * boot_se

boot_summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "Quantity": ["Bootstrapped SE", "Analytical SE", "95% CI lower", "95% CI upper"],
    "Value": [boot_se, analytic_se, ci_lower, ci_upper]
})

display(boot_summary)








Quantity
Value




0
Bootstrapped SE
0.017951


1
Analytical SE
0.018319


2
95% CI lower
0.017816


3
95% CI upper
0.088184







The bootstrap and analytical standard errors should be close. That tells us the resampling approach is producing a sensible estimate of uncertainty.

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
plt.hist(boot_thetas, bins=35)
plt.axvline(theta_hat, linestyle="--")
plt.title("Bootstrap Distribution of the Estimated Effect")
plt.xlabel(r"Bootstrapped $\hat{\theta}$")
plt.ylabel("Count")
plt.show()








A 95% confidence interval based on the bootstrap standard error runs from about ci_lower to ci_upper. If that interval stays above zero, it suggests CTA A likely performs better than CTA B.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Central Limit Theorem
The Central Limit Theorem says that as the sample size grows, the sampling distribution of the sample mean becomes approximately Normal. The same idea applies to the difference in sample means.

def simulate_theta(sample_n, reps=1000, pA=pi_A, pB=pi_B):
    out = np.empty(reps)
    for i in range(reps):
        out[i] = (
            np.random.binomial(1, pA, sample_n).mean()
            - np.random.binomial(1, pB, sample_n).mean()
        )
    return out

sample_sizes = [25, 50, 100, 500]
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(10, 8), sharex=True, sharey=True)

for ax, sample_n in zip(axes.flatten(), sample_sizes):
    vals = simulate_theta(sample_n)
    ax.hist(vals, bins=30)
    ax.axvline(theta_true, linestyle="--")
    ax.set_title(f"n = {sample_n}")
    ax.set_xlabel(r"$\hat{\theta}$")
    ax.set_ylabel("Count")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()








At small sample sizes, the histograms are rougher. As the sample size increases, they become smoother and more bell-shaped, illustrating the Central Limit Theorem.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Hypothesis Testing
To formally test whether the two CTAs perform differently, I test:
\[ H_0: = 0 \]
against
\[ H_1: \]
The test statistic is:
\[ z = \]

z_stat = theta_hat / analytic_se
p_value = 2 * (1 - norm.cdf(abs(z_stat)))

test_summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "Estimated effect": [pct(theta_hat)],
    "Standard error": [round(analytic_se, 4)],
    "z statistic": [round(z_stat, 3)],
    "p-value": [round(p_value, 4)]
})

display(test_summary)








Estimated effect
Standard error
z statistic
p-value




0
5.3%
0.0183
2.893
0.0038







A small p-value means the observed difference would be unlikely if the true difference were really zero.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The T-Test as a Regression
The two-sample test can also be written as a regression. Stack both groups into one dataset and estimate:
\[ Y_i = _0 + _1 D_i + _i \]
where \(D_i = 1\) for CTA A and \(D_i = 0\) for CTA B.

reg_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "y": np.concatenate([A, B]),
    "D": np.concatenate([np.ones(n), np.zeros(n)])
})

X = sm.add_constant(reg_df["D"])
model = sm.OLS(reg_df["y"], X).fit()

reg_summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "term": model.params.index,
    "estimate": np.round(model.params.values, 4),
    "std_error": np.round(model.bse.values, 4),
    "t_stat": np.round(model.tvalues.values, 3),
    "p_value": np.round(model.pvalues.values, 4)
})

display(reg_summary)

estimate
std_error
t_stat
p_value

The coefficient on the treatment indicator is the same treatment effect estimated earlier using the difference in means.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Problem with Peeking
Peeking means checking for significance repeatedly before the planned sample is complete. That inflates the false positive rate because each peek is another chance to get a statistically significant result by luck.

np.random.seed(999)

def peek_once(n_total=1000, p=0.20, alpha=0.05, step=100):
    A0 = np.random.binomial(1, p, n_total)
    B0 = np.random.binomial(1, p, n_total)

    for current_n in range(step, n_total + 1, step):
        A_sub = A0[:current_n]
        B_sub = B0[:current_n]

        theta_sub = A_sub.mean() - B_sub.mean()
        se_sub = np.sqrt(
            (A_sub.mean() * (1 - A_sub.mean())) / current_n +
            (B_sub.mean() * (1 - B_sub.mean())) / current_n
        )

        if se_sub == 0:
            continue

        z_sub = theta_sub / se_sub
        p_sub = 2 * (1 - norm.cdf(abs(z_sub)))

        if p_sub < alpha:
            return True
    return False

peek_results = np.array([peek_once() for _ in range(10000)])
false_positive_rate = peek_results.mean()

peek_summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "Nominal false positive rate": ["5.0%"],
    "Empirical false positive rate with peeking": [pct(false_positive_rate)]
})

display(peek_summary)








Nominal false positive rate
Empirical false positive rate with peeking




0
5.0%
20.0%







When repeated testing raises the false positive rate above 5%, it means you are more likely to declare a winner even when no real difference exists.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Conclusion
This A/B testing example shows how frequentist tools help translate webpage experiments into statistical decisions. The difference in means estimates the treatment effect, large-sample theory explains why that estimate becomes reliable, and standard errors and hypothesis tests quantify uncertainty. Just as importantly, the peeking example shows that statistical validity depends not only on the formulas we use, but also on following the experimental design we planned in advance.

title: Paola Montejo
text: Welcome to my website!

title: About
text: About this site
